coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin NCDs/BiOBr with oxygen vacancies for efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracyclines: Revealing the triple role of NCDs in the synthesis

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants is a widely used technology. Constructing oxygen vacancies, compounding other materials, and adjusting semiconductor morphology are effective ways to promote the performance of photocatalysis. In this study, ultrathin NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets with excellent photocatalytic ability were prepared. The triple role of NCDs in NCDs/BiOBr synthesis was revealed: controlling the thickness of the nanosheets, increasing the oxygen vacancy concentration, and regulating the band gap structure. Rhodamine B and tetracycline were degraded by the samples in visible light to examine their photocatalytic properties. The samples with the highest electron transfer efficiency were verified by optical and electrochemical characterization. As-synthesized NCDs/BiOBr catalysts exhibited good degradation in different water samples，indicating the ability of the samples for practical applications. In addition, the intermediates and possible degradation pathways of the prepared catalysts for the degradation of TC processes were also analyzed in depth. The electron transfer pathway and photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs/BiOBr were proposed in combination with free radical detection and energy band calculation.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin NCDs/BiOBr with oxygen vacancies for efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracyclines: Revealing the triple role of NCDs in the synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants is a widely used technology. Constructing oxygen vacancies, compounding other materials, and adjusting semiconductor morphology are effective ways to promote the performance of photocatalysis. In this study, ultrathin NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets with excellent photocatalytic ability were prepared. The triple role of NCDs in NCDs/BiOBr synthesis was revealed: controlling the thickness of the nanosheets, increasing the oxygen vacancy concentration, and regulating the band gap structure. Rhodamine B and tetracycline were degraded by the samples in visible light to examine their photocatalytic properties. The samples with the highest electron transfer efficiency were verified by optical and electrochemical characterization. As-synthesized NCDs/BiOBr catalysts exhibited good degradation in different water samples，indicating the ability of the samples for practical applications. In addition, the intermediates and possible degradation pathways of the prepared catalysts for the degradation of TC processes were also analyzed in depth. The electron transfer pathway and photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs/BiOBr were proposed in combination with free radical detection and energy band calculation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water resources are undoubtedly an essential resource for the survival and the existence of life [1]. Water resources face severe pollution from organic dyes and antibiotics [2], making water treatment an urgent task. Currently, biodegradation, adsorption, electrolysis, and photocatalysis are being investigated for water pollution, which are optimized for the effective treatment of wastewater [3]. Among these methods, Semiconductor photocatalysts with strong redox ability [4,5], good stability and sustainability have become the most important research targets [6,7]. However, photocatalysts are limited by their low utilization of photons and poor visible-light absorption [8].

As a typical two-dimensional nanomaterial, BiOBr exhibits a unique layer-like structure, which is widely used for photocatalysis [9]. It is characterized by [Bi2O2]2+ slabs along the (0 0 1) direction and double intercalating bromine atom slabs [10]. Photogenerated carriers are more dynamic due to the layered structure that facilitates the formation of electrostatic fields. However, BiOBr has a limited optical absorption wavelength and the complexation of electron-hole pairs remains severe [11]. Recent studies have indicated that constructing oxygen vacancies (OVs) can effectively improve this problem. OVs can preferentially capture separated electrons [12], suppression the complexation of electron-hole pairs, decrease the semiconductor band gap [13], enhance surface oxygen adsorption and thus effectively improving the degradation capacity [14]. A further benefit of OVs is that they enhance the ability of the catalyst to capture visible light. BiOBr-OVs can be constructed by high-temperature treatment [15], UV radiation [16], polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) induction, and coordination with different dissolvents like isopropanol, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran, and ethylene glycol (EG) [17]. Moreover, control of the OVs via the adjustment of the EG-water system is a simpler and milder method [18,19]. However, it is not enough to modify the utilization of photogenerated carriers and broaden its photo-response range by constructing oxygen vacancies.

In photocatalysis, carbon dots (CDs) have unique physical, chemical and biological properties [20]. CDs themselves have good optical effects and can provide more active surface sites for semiconductors and can also store electrons [21]. In catalysts compounded with CDs, electrons can migrate to the CDs while the CDs themselves can provide electrons. Therefore, the combination of carbon dots and semiconductors can increase the carrier separation efficiency and promote photocatalysis [22]. In addition, it has been found that CDs can be used to promote photocatalysis in concert with OVs [23,24]. Meanwhile, CDs may have the effect of changing the morphology of the catalyst [25–27]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have higher quantum yields and can further enhance the absorption capability of semiconductor photocatalysts in the full spectral wavelength band [28]. Carbon dots have excellent biocompatibility, so they can be easily combined with other substances [29]. The surface of carbon dots is rich in functional groups such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups, which can greatly improve their catalytic activity when combined with other catalysts [30]. Some materials can improve their morphology after adding carbon dots, such as increasing their specific surface area and exfoliating nanosheets [25]. When compounded with photocatalytic materials, carbon dots can be used as electron donors and acceptors to improve their photocatalytic ability [31]. Therefore, NCDs can be synthesized and introduced into semiconductor systems to enhance photocatalytic performance.

A novel controlled synthesis of BiOBr using carbon sites is proposed. NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets with OVs were synthesized using a system comprising an NCDs solution and EG without a surfactant. The photocatalytic degradation of BiOBr was considerably augmented by the combined effect of NCDs and OVs. The effects of NCDs solution and EG system on OVs were investigated in depth. Thinner BiOBr nanosheets were generated by introducing NCDs, and the band gap of NCDs/BiOBr was decreased, while the catalyst was tested to have more oxygen vacancies. The electron-hole separation and complexation changes were analyzed by the characterization of their optical and electrochemical properties. In contrast to BiOBr, NCDs/BiOBr demonstrated substantially improved photocatalysis of RhB and tetracycline. The prepared photocatalysts showed good degradation in different water bodies. The products of tetracycline degradation by this catalyst and the possible degradation pathways were also tested.

The balsa wood powder was collected from Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, China. The water as a real water sample was obtained from the Majia River, Harbin. Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, 99%), potassium bromide (KBr, 99%), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, 99%), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O, 99%), Rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline (TC, C22H24N2O8, 98%) and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were bought from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. All reagents above were of analytically pure grade and used without additional treatments.

NCDs are prepared by hydrothermal method using cellulose as raw material. Usually, 0.5 g of cellulose (balsa) is dispersed in 30 ml of deionized water by magnetic stirring. Next, added 0.2 g of citric acid (CA) to the cellulose dispersion, then added 0.2 ml of ethylenediamine (EDA) dropwise. The prepared mixture was placed into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor hydrothermally at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling, use a filter with 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove macromolecular impurities for the next step. To obtain a purified solution of NCDs, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove impurities. The purified NCDs solution was freeze-dried into NCDs powder for later synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BiOBr and NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Different NCDs solution concentrations were prepared by dissolving each of 3 mg, 5 mg, and 7 mg of NCDs powder in 20 ml water. First, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3 ·5 H2O was added to 20 ml EG and then sonicated. This was followed by adding 10 ml of different concentrations of NCDs solution, stirring well and then adding 1 mmol KBr. The mixed solution first turned brown and became turbid with stirring for 1 h, at which time it was transferred to a 50 ml reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. As a final step, the samples were washed with deionized water to remove surface debris, at which point they were placed in an incubator and dried at 60 °C. Thus, a series of BiOBr nanosheets containing different NCDs contents are prepared, referred to as 3NCDs/BiOBr, 5NCDs/BiOBr, and 7NCDs/BiOBr. Pure BiOBr nanosheets was synthesized by replacing 10 ml of NCDs solution with 10 ml of deionized water.
The details are characterized in the supporting information.

The CHI 760E workstation three-electrode system was used to test the catalyst. The counter electrode can use a platinum wire, and Ag/AgCl adapted to a neutral solution is chosen as a reference. In order to report the transient photocurrent and EIS of the samples, Na2SO4 with a concentration of 0.5 M was used as the electrolyte. 5 mg of the catalyst was sonicated and dissolved in a composition of 100 µL of ethanol and water, at which time a drop of 50 µL of Nafionr solution was added. After mixing well, a portion was removed and drop coated on FTO sheet, dried and applied to a working electrode. To measure the Mott-Schottky (MS) curve of the sample, Na2SO4 solution with a concentration of 1 M was used as the electrolyte.

Photocatalytic experiments were performed to determine the photodegradation of NCDs/BiOBr using a 300w visible light source xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) purified of RhB and TC in water. Disperse 30 mg of catalyst in 50 ml of 10 mg/L RhB solution and 50 ml of 20 mg/L tetracycline solution, respectively. The adsorption was stabilized by magnetic agitation in the dark for 30 min. A small amount of liquid was taken every 10 min after the start of light, filtered through a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) and injected into a centrifuge tube. The obtained RhB and TC solutions were detected utilizing UV-Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950), and the maximum absorption wavelengths of RhB and TC solutions were measured at 554 and 356 nm. The performance of the catalysts for practical applications was investigated by degrading contaminants in deionized water, tap or river water. The intermediates of NCDs/BiOBr degradation of TC were determined by LC-MS (Ultimate 3000 UHPLC-Q Exactive, US) and the degradation pathways were inferred.

Identification of the active substance that plays a predominant role by adding free radical inhibitors. Concentrations of 1 mM isopropyl alcohol (IPA), EDTA-2Na and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as scavengers of hydroxyl radicals (·OH), vacancies (h+) and superoxide radicals (·
O
2
−
), respectively.

The morphology of the catalysts was observed by SEM and TEM. All sample microscopic morphologies exhibit stacked two-dimensional nanosheet structures in SEM images. Nevertheless, the thicknesses of these catalysts were varied, with the thickness of the nanosheets without added carbon dots being significantly greater than that of 5NCDs/BiOBr ( Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b). During the synthesis, the higher viscosity of ethylene glycol can control the movement rate of precursor ions in the NCDs/ethylene glycol mixture solution [32], thus inhibiting the growth of BiOBr crystals. Therefore, we removed the influence of the EGs themselves on the experiment by the control variables method. In the experiment we controlled the same amount of ethylene glycol in each component and changed only the amount of NCDs added (concentration of aqueous solution of NCDs). Finally, based on the experimental results we determined the effect of NCDs on the thickness of the synthesized NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets. As can be observed by SEM (Fig. S1a-b), the samples with the added NCDs become thinner. The thickness of the final synthesized NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets gradually decreased as the number of carbon dots increased, so we synthesized ultrathin NCDs/BiOBr nanosheets. The EDS images of 5NCDs/BiOBr and related elemental information are shown in Fig. S1c-g, which indicates that the samples comprise C, O, Br, and Bi, and confirming the successful synthesis of BiOBr.

As Fig. 1 f and Fig. 1 g, the thicknesses of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr were precisely characterized by AFM. After calculation (Fig. 1 h), the thickness of BiOBr is estimated to be ∼25 nm, and the thickness of 5NCDs/BiOBr is estimated to be ∼4 nm. Ultrathin BiOBr nanosheets were produced by adding carbon dots, which is likely due to the stripping effect of NCDs [26,27], which disintegrated the structure in the addition of NCDs during the synthesis. Thin BiOBr nanosheets have been reported to exhibit a high carrier density and charge-transfer efficiency [33]. This is one of the reasons for the higher efficiency of 5NCDs/BiOBr in photodegradation of pollutants.

The microstructure was deeply investigated by TEM (Fig. 1c), and the pictures confirmed that the sample was composed of nanosheets. Moreover, 5NCDs/BiOBr is transparent, indicative of the successful synthesis of ultrathin nanosheets. HRTEM was employed to observe the 5NCDs/BiOBr microstructure: Dark black dots, corresponding to NCDs, are clearly visible in Fig. 1d. Hence, the size of the NCDs obtained after dialysis is uniform, which are successfully fixed on the BiOBr nanosheets and well dispersed. In Fig. 1e, 0.28 nm are the lattice spacings of the BiOBr (110) crystal plane [34] and 0.32 nm are the lattice spacings of NCDs [35]. It demonstrated the successful synthesis of NCDs/BiOBr.

XRD was conducted to precisely identify the crystal structures of the catalysts spiked with different amounts of NCDs ( Fig. 2a). The positions of these diffraction peaks correspond to the pure tetragonal phase of BiOBr (JCPDS No. 73–2061) [36]. As seen in Fig. 2a, there is a slight decrease in the intensity of the XRD diffraction peaks of BiOBr with the increase in the amount of NCDs doping. This may be due to the increase in the number of carbon dots leading to an increase in the defects (oxygen vacancies) of BiOBr, which affects the intensity of the XRD peaks. The XRD patterns of the NCDs are shown in Fig. S8, it can be seen that there is a diffraction peak corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal face at 22.1°[37]. However, the addition of NCDs did not change the position of the diffraction peaks corresponding to BiOBr. By the addition of NCDs, the positions of the diffraction peaks corresponding to BiOBr are not changed. This indicates that the solution-assisted synthesis of NCDs/BiOBr by NCDs had not change the primitive crystal structure of BiOBr. Nevertheless, samples with NCDs added in XRD only showed the characteristic peaks of BiOBr because the amount of NCDs added was small.

The OVs in the samples were analyzed by EPR spectroscopy at room temperature. From the EPR results (Fig. 2b), the samples synthesized with the help of NCDs solutions (3NCDs/BiOBr, 5NCDs/BiOBr, 7NCDs/BiOBr) were observed with strong OV signals at the g= 2.003 position. In contrast, the OVs signals detected by BiOBr prepared using a mixture of water and ethylene glycol solvents were lower than those of the samples doped with NCDs. For comparison, samples synthesized with pure water and pure ethylene glycol (BiOBr-water, BiOBr-eg) were prepared. It can be seen that the signal of OVs is almost undetectable in BiOBr-water, while the signal intensity of OVs of the sample synthesized by pure EG is significantly lower. It indicates that the OVs of the samples are mainly formed due to the interaction between NCDs solution and the ethylene glycol solution system. Water is the solvent for the NCD solution; hence, OV formation is thought to be similar to the preparation of BiOBr containing OVs using the water–EG mixed solvent, and NCDs play a key role. The specific explanation is as follows. In the synthesis of BiOBr, the introduction of NCDs solution reduced the viscosity of the glycol solution and regulated the kinetics of Bi+ solvolysis. The nucleation and growth of BiOBr was achieved at room temperature [38,39], enabling the complexation of NCDs onto BiOBr. The subsequent high-temperature solvent heat enhanced the interaction of NCDs with BiOBr, stretching the Bi-O bond and leading to breakage and, eventually, the formation of surface OVs. There is also a possibility that the high temperature solvent heat stabilizes the OVs of NCDs, resulting in more OVs of NCDs/BiOBr [40,41]. In conclusion, the number of OVs in the synthesized BiOBr increases with the increase of NCDs doping. The regulation of OVs in BiOBr can be achieved by adjusting the concentration of NCDs solution.

The elemental components of the obtained BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr catalysts and their chemical states were further examined using XPS. Fig. 3e shows peaks of Bi, O, Br, and C in the full scan of XPS. The specific peaks of C1s on 284.8 eV were used as calibration standards. The binding energies of the two main peaks in Bi 4 f of Fig. 3a correspond to 159.4 eV and 164.7 eV for Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2 belonging to Bi3+, respectively. 5NCDs/BiOBr have Bi 4 f spectra at 159.6 eV and 164.9 eV, while these peaks are shifted in comparison with BiOBr. This shift indicates the presence of interactions between BiOBr catalysts and NCDs [42], as well as the possible presence of OVs in the [Bi2O2]2+ layer of 5NCDs/BiOBr during synthesis [24]. The Br 3d spectrum in Fig. 3b can be fitted to two independent Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2 peaks with binding energies at 69.5 eV and 68.4 eV. It indicates that the Br element in the catalyst is in the
Br
−
form. As shown in Fig. 3c, the O1s spectra of the catalysts can be obtained with peaks of ∼530.0, 531.3 and 532.7 eV for peak splitting. This indicates that there are three different forms of oxygen in BiOBr, which are the lattice oxygen that constitutes BiOBr, oxygen vacancies generated in the synthesis and chemisorbed oxygen such as -OH [43]. The increase of oxygen vacancies in 5NCDs/BiOBr leads to a more prominent peak area of OVs. Fig. 3d shows the C1s spectrum of 5NCDs/BiOBr, and after fitting, three peaks with binding energies of 284.8, 285.7, and 287.7 eV are obtained, corresponding to C-C/CC, oxygenated carbon, and nitrous carbon [44], respectively. Fig. S5 shows the spectra of N1s of 5NCDs/BiOBr, fitted to obtain a peak with a binding energy of 400.0 eV [20,45]. This peak originates from the -NH2 group in the NCDs. This indicates that the synthesized carbon dots are nitrogen-doped carbon dots and further demonstrates the successful synthesis of 5NCDs/BiOBr.

The FT-IR spectra of the prepared NCDs, BiOBr, and 5NCDs/BiOBr were recorded. From the spectra of NCDs (Fig. 3f), the peak located at 2921 cm−1 belongs to C-H stretching vibration [46], and the characteristic peak at 1650 cm−1 corresponds to the CO signal [45]. The N-H bending vibration at 1534 cm−1 and the C-N bending vibration at 1386 cm−1 prove the presence of the amine group [37]. It indicates the successful preparation of NCDs. The FT-IR spectra of BiOBr, 5NCDs/BiOBr have of Bi-O at 517 cm−1. 2926 cm−1 band in the spectrum of 5NCDs/BiOBr is attributed to the C-H stretching vibration, which proves the successful synthesis of 5NCDs/BiOBr. Owing to the low amount of NCDs, the moieties of NCDs could not be observed in the FT-IR spectrum.

The structural properties of the photocatalysts were investigated by BET tests. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of 5NCDs/BiOBr and BiOBr (Fig. S6) were obtained, and the curves in the figure were well closed. The specific surface area of 5NCDs/BiOBr was further obtained from the calculation as 21.44 m2/g, while that of BiOBr was 14.43 m2/g. The specific surface area of BiOBr became larger after the addition of NCDs. This may be due to the fact that the nanosheets added with NCDs become thinner and larger, and also the NCDs themselves have excellent adsorption properties. The 5NCDs/BiOBr with higher specific surface area can adsorb more active substances as well as reactants in the photocatalytic reaction and have more active sites. Therefore, 5NCDs/BiOBr have better photocatalytic performance than pure BiOBr.

The NCDs solution has a distinct green emission under UV irradiation, and its UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded within the excitation of 200–800 (Fig. S2). NCDs has two absorption peaks at 241 nm and 342 nm, which belong to the π-π * and n-π * transition of conjugated CO or CN[47], respectively. An important factor affecting photocatalysis is the optical property of the photocatalysts; hence, the optical properties are evaluated by UV-DRS spectra. BiOBr exhibits an intrinsic absorption of 408 nm ( Fig. 4a). NCDs/BiOBr have strong absorption peaks in the UV band, and also have better effects in the visible spectral region [48]. As the NCD content increases, the material exhibits higher absorption intensity in the range of 400–750 nm. This result is attributed to the optical properties of the NCDs and the increase in OVs due to the NCDs. In addition, the band gap of the sample was estimated using the Tauc curve by the following equation: α (hν) = A (hν - Eg) n/2 [49]. Here, α, hν, A, Eg, and n represent the absorption coefficient, photon energy, constant, and band gap energy, respectively, which are constantly determined by the type of optical leap of the semiconductor material [50]. n is equal to 4 as the fundamental absorption of BiOBr is an indirect leap between bands. According to the equation, the band gaps of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr are calculated to be 2.99 and 2.77 eV, respectively. With the increase in the NCDs, the forbidden band width of BiOBr is gradually shortened. Therefore, 5NCDs/BiOBr require less excitation light energy compared to BiOBr.

The carrier density in a semiconductor material determines its catalytic activity. In order to find the electron shift path, the energy band position of the sample is crucial. Therefore, the Mott-Schottky diagram of the catalyst was measured by electrochemical methods. As the slopes of BiOBr and NCDs/BiOBr in Fig. 4c are both positive, so the prepared catalysts are both n-type semiconductors [51]. The flat-band potentials of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr are − 0.55 and − 0.58 eV, respectively, obtained from Mott-Schottky plots. According to the literature [52,53], the flat-band potentials are very close to the conduction bands of n-type semiconductors. Thus the conduction band potentials of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr are − 0.55 and − 0.58 eV, respectively. Therefore, the VB top potentials of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr are calculated to be 2.44 and 2.19 eV (EVB = Eg + ECB). As an important indication of photocatalytic performance, the charge carrier density is inversely proportional to the slope of the Mott-Schottky curve [33]. The slope of 5NCDs/BiOBr is smaller (Fig. 4c), so the charge carrier density of 5NCDs/BiOBr is larger relative to BiOBr. It indicates that under the same conditions, 5NCDs/BiOBr can produce more active substances that contribute to photocatalysis and exhibit higher photocatalytic activity.

Optical and electrochemical tests were performed to evaluate the photon utilization as well as the electron transfer efficiency of the catalyst [54]. The Nyquist plot in the EIS of BiOBr revealed a considerably larger arc radius than that observed for 5NCDs/BiOBr ( Fig. 5a), and the arc radii of the samples with added NCDs are less than those of the original samples [55]. This result indicates that the photogenerated carriers of the catalysts without the addition of carbon dots have a higher resistance when migrating, which is due to the intrinsic charge recombination occurring in the catalysts. In contrast, the 5NCDs/BiOBr had a small resistance for carrier transfer and the best conversion efficiency [56]. Therefore, the combination of a small amount of NCDs in the composite material can make the electron transfer easier.

The periodic on/off photocurrent responses of BiOBr and 5NCDs/BiOBr were measured under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 5b). 5NCDs/BiOBr exhibits the greatest photocurrent density, and the catalysts with the added NCDs were stronger than the original ones. This further demonstrates that NCDs can be used as carriers of electrons [57], making the separation of photogenerated charges much more efficient. This result is also attributed to the increase in the visible-light absorption caused by the addition of NCDs. Therefore, with the increase in the NCDs content, the photocurrent intensity of the catalysts gradually increases.

PL spectra of the BiOBr and NCDs/BiOBr were detected to observe the post-irradiation carrier composite changes of the samples [58]. It was found that the strongest fluorescence peak of the sample was obtained using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, so this excitation wavelength was used to observe the change in the intensity of the catalyst fluorescence peak (Fig. 5c). It can be seen that the fluorescence peak of 5NCDs/BiOBr is the lowest, and the fluorescence intensity of the samples with NCDs is lower than that of the initial BiOBr. This result indicates that the incorporation of NCDs into BiOBr can reduce the composite of photogenerated carriers, thereby improving the charge separation efficiency [59]. The results of PL spectral analysis are consistent with those obtained by electrochemical measurements and photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of RhB and TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the photodegradation pollutant performance of the prepared materials, hard-to-degrade TC and the popularly used RhB dyes were chosen as the target pollutants. The concentration of the tetracycline solution was almost unchanged in the blank experiment, indicating that TC is photostable and does not degrade naturally. Fig. 6a shows the degradation curves of TC by different catalysts within 60 min of xenon lamp irradiation. Among all catalysts, the degradation efficiency of 5NCDs/BiOBr for TC was about 92%, which was much higher than that of BiOBr (63%), 3NCDs/BiOBr (79%), 7NCDs/BiOBr (86%). The degradation capacity increases with the compounding of NCDs content, but the excessive amount of NCDs leads to the decrease of catalytic capacity. This is probably due to the fact that excess NCDs reduces the active sites exposed to the semiconductor photocatalyst surface. A kinetic fit to the TC degradation curve was performed to further compare the kinetic constants of the four catalysts for degradation. A pseudo-first-order kinetic equation was used: -ln (Ct/C0) = kt, where C is the concentration of TC degraded by NCDs/BiOBr at a certain time of light exposure, C0 is the initial concentration of TC, and k is the apparent rate constant [60]. Apparently, 5NCDs/BiOBr had the best apparent rate constant of 0.042 min−1. In addition, the performance of NCDs/BiOBr for the Photodegradation of RhB was analyzed. Without a catalyst (Fig. 6c), negligible degradation of RhB was observed over 70 min of visible light irradiation. 5NCDs/BiOBr had the best photocatalytic ability, purifying 98% of RhB within 70 min, while BiOBr degraded only 76% of RhB. By fitting the primary reaction kinetics of the samples, 5NCDs/BiOBr exhibits the highest apparent rate constant of 0.056 min−1. The photocatalysts need to be stable considering the secondary pollution problem of practical applications [61]. To test the stability of the composites, the degradation ability of the 5NCDs/BiOBr composites against TC and RhB after 5 cycles was investigated. As shown in Figs. 6e and 6f, the TC and RhB removal rates could still reach 86% and 91% after 5 consecutive cycles without any significant decrease. This indicates that our photocatalysts have high stability. In order to investigate the mineralization ability of the samples to TC, the removal rate of the samples to TOC was tested. The concentrations of TOC measured during the degradation of TC by 5NCDs/BiOBr in 120 min are shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen that the concentration of TOC gradually decreases as the degradation time increases, and finally 50% of TOC can be degraded at 120 min. From this, it can be obtained that the mineralization rate of the sample on TC gradually increases, and finally it can be decomposed into CO2, NO3-, NH4+ and other small molecules.

Usually, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of a sample is highly dependent on the degradation environment, such as water quality, pH, air quality, and so on. To determine the influence of the configuration solution pH on the purification capacity of TC and RhB, the pH of the reaction system was adjusted to 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 by adding HCl or NaOH. With the pH change, the purification efficiency of TC was almost unaffected ( Fig. 7a), indicating that the catalyst was stability to the change of pH. When the pH was adjusted to 11, the degradation efficiency of the catalyst for RhB decreased to 82% (Fig. 7b), indicating that alkaline environment is not conducive to the degradation of RhB. In addition, the impacts of different water sources for the targeted degradates were discussed. Degradation of TC was investigated in deionized water, tap water, and river water, and the degradation rates reach 92%, 82%, and 74%, respectively (Fig. 7c). After irradiation for 60 min, the removal rates of RhB in deionized water, tap water and river water containing RhB reached 98%, 89% and 86%, respectively (Fig. 7d). The degradation efficiency became lower because the river water contained more impurities and some ions that affected the photocatalytic activity, but still degraded most of the TC and RhB. This shows the value of the prepared catalysts for developable practical applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intermediate products and degradation pathways of TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The LC-MS technique was used to analyze the structure and type of the obtained products and to speculate on the possible pathways of their degradation. The initial substrate (TC) gives the protonated excimer ion peak [M+H]+ as m/z 445 [62]. The first possible reaction channel shows that firstly the TC is attacked by ·
O
2
−
, H+, and ·OH radicals. The oxidative hydroxylation of the “A” ring gives the product m/z 461, which is followed by oxidative dehydrogenation to form the quinone-type product m/z 459. The second possible reaction channel is the substance m/z 461 obtained by the oxidative hydroxylation of the "A" ring of TC. Reoxidation removes a methyl group from dimethylamine to give the substance m/z 447. Then, the m/z 449 is obtained by oxidation opening of the “D” ring. The oxidative dehydrogenation of m/z 461 resulted in the formation of the quinone product m/z 459. Further degradation was manifested as demethylation to produce the product m/z 445. Finally, further demethylation gives the product m/z 431. The third possible reaction pathway is that due to the action of ·
O
2
−
, H+, and ·OH free-radicals, TC demethylates the dimethylamine to obtain the product m/z 431. Further demethylation of dimethylamine gives the product m/z 417. The product m/z 433 was obtained by oxidative hydroxylation of the “A” ring. The quinone product m/z 431 was formed by oxidative dehydrogenation. The above product m/z 431 is further obtained by oxidation ring-opening to get product m/z 303. These substances undergo ring-opening and oxidation reactions to give H2O, CO2, NO3-, NH4+, and thus achieve complete degradation of the compounds Fig. 8.

To verify the role of active substances in the degradation of RhB and TC, free radical scavenging experiments were conducted. The catalytic effects of vacancies (h+), superoxide radicals (·
O
2
−
), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) were inhibited using EDTA-2Na, BQ, and IPA [63], respectively. The inclusion of EDTA-2Na to the solution before the start of the reaction resulted in the final degradation of only 46% of RhB and 24% of TC by 5NCDs/BiOBr. By replacing EDTA-2Na with BQ, only 58% of RhB and 36% of TC were degraded at the end of the reaction (Fig. 5a). This result indicates that h+ and ·
O
2
−
play a major role in photocatalysis. In contrast, the catalyst after adding IPA still degraded 91% of RhB and 84% of TC, indicating that ·OH is not the main active substance. For the detection of active species produced by the catalyst, ESR spin-traps technique were employed. DMPO acts as a radical trap to capture the ·
O
2
−
and ·OH signals. There was no significant peak in the ·OH signal in the dark or light (Fig. S4). This result indicates that 5NCDs/BiOBr cannot produce ·OH. This is because the top of the VB of 5NCDs/BiOBr is below the redox potential of H2O/ ·OH (2.33 eV). An ·
O
2
−
signal was not detected in the absence of light, and four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1, corresponding to the ·
O
2
−
signal, were detected under illumination for 5 min ( Fig. 9b). The stronger ·
O
2
−
signal was detected after 10 min of illumination, indicating that the 5NCDs/BiOBr catalyst produced a high amount of ·
O
2
−
. The ESR results further confirmed that ·
O
2
−
is the dominant active substance in the reaction process.

Based on the above results, the photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs and OVs together was speculated and analyzed. On the one hand, the light-absorbing ability of NCDs/BiOBr is significantly enhanced by the presence of NCDs, and the separation efficiency of the electrons in NCDs/BiOBr is also significantly enhanced, which improves the photocatalytic ability. On the other hand, the abundant OVs cause the NCDs/BiOBr to generate defective energy levels, which inhibit the electron-hole complexation. At the same time, it can provide active sites to adsorb more active species, thus improving the photocatalytic activity.

The electron transfer pathway is the direct migration of electrons from the VB of BiOBr to the CB of BiOBr, which generates holes (h+) in the VB of BiOBr. Due to the presence of OVs in BiOBr, the electrons in the CB of BiOBr continue to migrate to the defect energy levels induced by the OVs [64]. Due to the strong interaction between the NCDs and BiOBr, the electrons will continue to migrate to the NCDs [13], thus increasing the efficiency of electron-hole separation. The electrons on the NCDs can activate O2 to produce ·
O
2
−
. Finally, the TC and RhB macromolecules on the catalyst surface are degraded by the active species ·
O
2
−
, h+
Fig. 10.

In summary, NCDs/BiOBr ultrathin nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies (OVs) using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) solutions were successfully synthesized. The addition of NCDs makes the BiOBr nanosheets thinner. At the same time, NCDs induce BiOBr to produce large amounts of OVs and adsorb large amounts of O2. Subsequently, photogenerated electrons captured by OVs activate O2 to ·
O
2
−
. The Z- scheme electron transfer mechanism formed by NCDs and BiOBr greatly enhances the separation efficiency of electrons and holes. Based on this triple effect of NCDs, the efficiency of NCDs/BiOBr for the visible-light degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline (TC) was considerably enhanced. Even in different water samples, NCDs/BiOBr exhibited good degradation performance. In addition, the degradation pathways of TCs and free radicals, which play a major role, were analyzed in depth. The synthesis method herein simultaneously utilized all three functions of NCDs. The use of carbon dots to improve catalyst morphology while inducing oxygen vacancy generation is hoped to be applied to other photocatalysts to produce better performing and more functional composites.
